commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=a908f8e24926cfeb9a6c6c61994836ecbe60679e)
commands.review_summary:
The PR gates async and sync suspicious-config-read dedup marker writes on `restoredFromBackup` and adds retry tests for a failed backup copy followed by a successful retry.

Reproducibility: Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue? Only at the internal-helper level: current main source shows failed helper restores can record the suspicious signature, and the PR tests model that path. I do not see a high-confidence runtime reproduction because the helper is not called by current-main config reads.
commands.review_followup: Maintainers need to decide whether suspicious-read backup recovery should be wired back into runtime config reads or whether the stale helper should be removed; automation should not choose that boundary.

target.title: fix(config): do not suppress recovery retry after failed backup restore
target.body:
## Summary

`maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` (and its sync variant) unconditionally record `lastObservedSuspiciousSignature` in the config health state after attempting a backup restore, even when `restoredFromBackup` is `false` (i.e., `copyFile` failed with EACCES, disk full, etc.).

The dedup guard in `resolveConfigReadRecoveryContext` (line 495) compares the current suspicious signature against the stored one and returns `null` (skip recovery) when they match. This means a single failed restore permanently suppresses all future recovery attempts for that config file, silently accepting the clobbered config on every subsequent launch.

## Fix

Wrap the `lastObservedSuspiciousSignature` recording in `if (restoredFromBackup)` so the dedup guard only activates when the backup was actually restored. Applied to both the async path (line 683) and the sync path (line 793).

When the restore fails, the signature is not recorded, so the next launch retries recovery. Once a retry succeeds, the signature is recorded normally and dedup resumes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Failed config backup restore permanently suppresses future recovery attempts via unconditional dedup signature recording.

Real environment tested: macOS, Node 24, pnpm, Vitest 4.1.7, from a fresh `upstream/main` worktree.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
cd /tmp/fix-config-recovery-retry
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/io.observe-recovery.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Evidence after fix:
```
 ✓ records copyFile failure instead of falsely claiming restore succeeded 2ms
 ✓ sync recovery records copyFileSync failure instead of falsely claiming restore succeeded 2ms
 ✓ retries recovery on next launch after a failed copyFile restore 3ms
 ✓ sync recovery retries on next launch after a failed copyFileSync restore 3ms
 ✓ dedupes repeated suspicious hashes 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
```

The two new tests (`retries recovery on next launch after a failed copyFile restore` and `sync recovery retries on next launch after a failed copyFileSync restore`) exercise the exact bug scenario:
1. Seed a good config with backup, clobber the config
2. First call: `copyFile` rejects with EACCES, `restoredFromBackup = false`
3. Verify 1 audit event with `restoredFromBackup: false`
4. Second call: `copyFile` succeeds (simulating next launch)
5. Verify 2 audit events, second with `restoredFromBackup: true`
6. Verify on-disk config is now the restored backup content

Before this patch, step 4 would return early at `resolveConfigReadRecoveryContext` (signature matched) and never attempt recovery, leaving only 1 audit event.

Observed result after fix: 20/20 tests pass. The existing `dedupes repeated suspicious hashes` test confirms that successful restores still record the signature for normal dedup. tsgo clean, oxlint 0 warnings.

What was not tested: Live `pnpm dev` with a real clobbered config file, because the recovery path requires a config with `gateway.mode` set and a `.bak` file with matching structure. The function is exercised via its injected `deps` interface using real filesystem operations (not mocks) in a temp directory.
target.author: sebtardif

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
